crop rotation: Crop rotation is the practice of planting different crops sequentially on the same plot of land to improve soil health, optimize nutrients in the soil, and combat pest and weed pressure. For example, planting a legume (like soybeans) that fixes nitrogen in the soil, followed by a nitrogen-demanding crop (like corn or wheat).
soil ph: Soil pH is a measure of the acidity or alkalinity of the soil. Most crops grow best in slightly acidic to neutral soil (pH 6.0 to 7.0). If soil is too acidic, agricultural lime (crushed limestone) can be added to raise the pH. If it is too alkaline, elemental sulfur can be applied to lower the pH.
nematode control: Nematodes are microscopic roundworms that can damage plant roots. Control methods include planting nematode-resistant crop varieties, practicing crop rotation with non-host plants, solarization (using the sun's heat to kill pests in the soil), and applying specific nematicides if organic methods fail.
